_meta.authoringNotes: Citations cross-verified against ATA Hypothyroidism Guidelines 2014 (Jonklaas/Bianco), Bahn 2011 hyperthyroidism guidelines, Pearce 2003 NEJM subacute thyroiditis review, Stagnaro-Green 2011 ATA postpartum thyroiditis guidelines. ICD-10 E06.

h1Title: Thyroiditis: Symptoms, Causes, Diagnosis, and Treatment
heroOverview: Thyroiditis is a family of inflammatory thyroid disorders that share a transient or sustained disturbance of thyroid hormone release, with Hashimoto thyroiditis the dominant cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-replete countries — affecting roughly 5% of the US population over a lifetime. The classic triphasic pattern of painless and subacute thyroiditis runs from a thyrotoxic phase lasting 4-8 weeks, through a hypothyroid phase of 2-6 months, to recovery in 80% of cases. Subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis follows viral upper respiratory infections by 2-8 weeks and produces an exquisitely painful, tender thyroid gland with high erythrocyte sedimentation rate. Postpartum thyroiditis affects 5-10% of women within 12 months of delivery. Drug-induced thyroiditis from amiodarone, lithium, interferon, and immune checkpoint inhibitors is increasingly common in oncology and cardiology populations. Treatment is supportive for transient forms and lifelong levothyroxine replacement for established Hashimoto disease.

keyStats.prevalence: Hashimoto thyroiditis: 5% lifetime US prevalence; subacute thyroiditis: 5 per 100,000 person-years (Mayo Clinic Olmsted County cohort)
keyStats.demographics: Women 5-10 times more affected than men across all forms except suppurative thyroiditis
keyStats.avgAge: Hashimoto onset peaks 30-50 years; subacute thyroiditis 40-50 years; postpartum thyroiditis within 12 months of delivery
keyStats.globalCases: Hashimoto is the leading cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient regions; iodine deficiency dominates elsewhere

definition: Thyroiditis (ICD-10: E06) is a group of inflammatory conditions of the thyroid gland that share altered hormone release as their final common pathway. Five major forms exist. Hashimoto (chronic autoimmune lymphocytic) thyroiditis is mediated by T-cell infiltration of the gland and circulating antibodies to thyroid peroxidase (TPO) and thyroglobulin, producing progressive follicular destruction and permanent hypothyroidism. Subacute (de Quervain or granulomatous) thyroiditis is a post-viral inflammatory disorder producing painful gland destruction, transient thyrotoxicosis, and usually full recovery. Painless (silent) and postpartum thyroiditis are autoimmune lymphocytic variants without pain, producing a triphasic pattern of thyrotoxicosis-hypothyroidism-recovery. Drug-induced thyroiditis follows exposure to amiodarone, lithium, interferon-alpha, tyrosine kinase inhibitors, and immune checkpoint inhibitors such as pembrolizumab. Acute (suppurative) thyroiditis is a rare bacterial infection of the thyroid producing fever, neck pain, and abscess formation, most often in children with congenital pyriform sinus fistula. Riedel thyroiditis is a rare fibrosing disorder with infiltrative woody enlargement. The condition is managed by endocrinologists in most countries, with ENT consultation needed for suppurative disease.

typesClassification.description: The most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient regions. Antibody-mediated destruction over years produces progressive failure. TPO antibodies are positive in 95% of cases. ICD-10 E06.3.

Painful post-viral granulomatous inflammation 2-8 weeks after upper respiratory infection. Causes transient thyrotoxicosis followed by hypothyroidism over 4-6 months, then recovery in 80-90%. ICD-10 E06.1.
Painless autoimmune thyroiditis occurring within 12 months of delivery in 5-10% of women. Often triphasic; recovery in 80%, with 20% progressing to permanent hypothyroidism. ICD-10 E06.5.
Sporadic autoimmune thyroiditis unrelated to pregnancy, with the same triphasic pattern as postpartum disease. Affects 1-5% of adults with autoimmune diathesis. ICD-10 E06.5.
Caused by amiodarone (Type 2 thyrotoxicosis), lithium, interferon-alpha, tyrosine kinase inhibitors, and immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab). ICD-10 E06.4.

Rare bacterial infection of the thyroid, typically by Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus species. Presents with fever, severe neck pain, and abscess. Children with pyriform sinus fistula are predisposed. ICD-10 E06.0.
Rare IgG4-related fibrosing disorder producing woody, stony-hard thyroid enlargement with compression of adjacent structures. Often part of multifocal fibrosclerosis. ICD-10 E06.5.

primarySymptoms: Painful, exquisitely tender thyroid gland with pain radiating to the ear or jaw in subacute thyroiditis — discomfort typically prevents the patient from buttoning a collar or turning the head.
Painless diffuse goiter that develops gradually over months or years in Hashimoto thyroiditis, often firm and rubbery on palpation rather than tender.
Symptoms of hyperthyroidism during the early thyrotoxic phase: palpitations, heat intolerance, tremor, weight loss despite increased appetite, anxiety, and loose stools.
Symptoms of hypothyroidism in the subsequent phase or in established Hashimoto disease: fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, hair thinning, and slowed cognition.
Postpartum mood changes, anxiety, palpitations, and weight loss between 1-6 months after delivery in the thyrotoxic phase of postpartum thyroiditis.
Persistent low-grade fever, malaise, and elevated inflammatory markers in subacute thyroiditis, often preceded by an upper respiratory infection 2-8 weeks earlier.
Sudden onset of severe anterior neck pain, fever, fluctuant mass, and dysphagia in acute suppurative thyroiditis — a surgical emergency.
Woody, stony-hard, painless thyroid enlargement with hoarseness, dysphagia, or stridor in Riedel thyroiditis from infiltration of adjacent structures.
Brittle nails, periorbital puffiness, and the slow pulse and delayed deep-tendon reflex relaxation classic for myxedema in advanced hypothyroidism.
Menstrual irregularities, infertility, and miscarriage in women with untreated autoimmune thyroiditis.
earlyWarningSigns: New-onset fatigue and weight gain with rising TSH on routine labs over 12-24 months — typical of evolving Hashimoto disease
Anterior neck pain radiating to the jaw or ear with low-grade fever 2-8 weeks after a viral illness — typical of subacute thyroiditis
Postpartum anxiety, palpitations, or unexpected weight loss within 12 months of delivery — postpartum thyroiditis affects 5-10% of women
New thyroid symptoms after starting amiodarone, lithium, interferon, or an immune checkpoint inhibitor — drug-induced thyroiditis is increasingly common
emergencySigns: High fever, severe anterior neck pain, fluctuant mass, and dysphagia — suppurative thyroiditis requires immediate ENT evaluation and surgical drainage
Severe stridor, dyspnea, or rapidly progressive dysphagia from a large thyroid mass — possible airway compromise requiring urgent intervention
Thyroid storm symptoms in the thyrotoxic phase: fever above 38.5°C, severe tachycardia, agitation, or altered mental status — endocrine emergency
Myxedema coma symptoms in advanced hypothyroidism: hypothermia, bradycardia, hypotension, and depressed consciousness — life-threatening, requires intensive care

causes.description: Genetic susceptibility (HLA-DR3, CTLA-4, PTPN22) combined with environmental triggers produces antibody-mediated and T-cell-mediated destruction of thyroid epithelium. TPO antibodies are detectable in 95% of Hashimoto cases and represent the most reliable diagnostic marker.

Subacute thyroiditis follows respiratory viral infections (mumps, coxsackie, adenovirus, influenza, SARS-CoV-2) by 2-8 weeks. The mechanism involves immune cross-reactivity rather than direct viral infection of follicular cells.

Postpartum thyroiditis occurs in 5-10% of women within 12 months of delivery. Pregnancy-induced immune tolerance reverses after delivery, unmasking autoimmune lymphocytic thyroiditis in genetically susceptible women.

Amiodarone (which contains 37% iodine by weight), lithium, interferon-alpha, tyrosine kinase inhibitors, and immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab) cause distinct patterns of thyroiditis. Checkpoint-inhibitor thyroiditis affects up to 20% of patients on combination therapy.

causes.cause: Bacterial infection of the thyroid gland
causes.description: Suppurative thyroiditis is most often caused by Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus species, or oral anaerobes spreading via pyriform sinus fistula in children. Less commonly seen in immunocompromised adults with hematogenous seeding.

Riedel thyroiditis is characterized by IgG4-positive plasma-cell infiltration and dense fibrosis of the thyroid and adjacent structures. It is part of the broader IgG4-related disease spectrum that includes autoimmune pancreatitis and retroperitoneal fibrosis.

Sudden iodine exposure (contrast media, amiodarone, dietary changes) can precipitate thyroiditis in autoimmune-prone individuals through the Jod-Basedow or Wolff-Chaikoff phenomena, producing transient thyrotoxicosis or hypothyroidism.

riskFactors.description: Women are 5-10 times more affected by autoimmune thyroiditis than men, reflecting X-chromosome immunogenetic factors and estrogen effects on immune regulation.

riskFactors.factor: Family history of autoimmune thyroid disease
riskFactors.description: First-degree relative with Hashimoto disease or Graves disease raises personal risk roughly 5-fold. Heritability estimates from twin studies reach 70%.

Type 1 diabetes, celiac disease, vitiligo, Addison disease, pernicious anemia, and rheumatoid arthritis all share immune susceptibility with autoimmune thyroiditis. Up to 40% of Hashimoto patients have a second autoimmune condition.
Subacute thyroiditis follows viral illness in 80% of cases. The link with SARS-CoV-2 has been confirmed in case series during 2020-2022.
Postpartum thyroiditis affects 5-10% of women within 12 months of delivery; risk rises to 25% in women with type 1 diabetes or pre-pregnancy TPO antibody positivity.

riskFactors.factor: Amiodarone, lithium, or checkpoint inhibitor therapy
riskFactors.description: Amiodarone causes thyroiditis in 5-10% of users; lithium in 5%; immune checkpoint inhibitors in 5-20% depending on regimen. Regular thyroid function monitoring is standard.

Excess iodine from supplements, kelp, or contrast media can trigger autoimmune thyroiditis in susceptible individuals. Effect is most pronounced in iodine-deficient populations newly exposed to high intake.
Selenium is required for glutathione peroxidase activity in the thyroid. Deficiency increases oxidative damage; supplementation modestly reduces TPO antibody titers in some randomized trials.
Smoking is paradoxically protective against Hashimoto disease but is a risk factor for Graves ophthalmopathy. Effect size is modest.
Congenital fistula from the pyriform sinus to the thyroid bed predisposes to recurrent acute suppurative thyroiditis in children. Surgical correction prevents recurrence.

affectedDemographics: Hashimoto thyroiditis affects 5% of US adults across a lifetime; subclinical disease much higher
Postpartum thyroiditis: 5-10% of women within 12 months of delivery
Subacute thyroiditis: 5 per 100,000 person-years; female:male ratio 4-5:1
Immune checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis: 5-20% of treated patients depending on regimen
diagnosisOverview: Diagnosis of thyroiditis combines clinical features, thyroid function tests, antibody screening, and selective imaging. TSH and free T4 establish whether the patient is currently hyperthyroid, euthyroid, or hypothyroid. TPO and thyroglobulin antibodies confirm autoimmune etiology — TPO antibodies are positive in 95% of Hashimoto disease and 60% of postpartum thyroiditis. Erythrocyte sedimentation rate above 50 mm/hour in a patient with painful neck swelling and thyrotoxicosis points to subacute thyroiditis; ESR is normal in painless and Hashimoto forms. The most decisive test for distinguishing thyroiditis from Graves disease in the thyrotoxic phase is radioactive iodine uptake (RAIU) — uptake is low (<5%) in thyroiditis because follicles are damaged and not synthesizing hormone, but high (>30%) in Graves disease. Thyroid ultrasound shows characteristic features: heterogeneous hypoechoic gland with diffusely increased vascularity (Hashimoto), focal hypoechoic regions with reduced vascularity (subacute), or stony-hard fibrosis with infiltrative pattern (Riedel). Fine-needle aspiration is reserved for atypical cases or suspected lymphoma — a known complication of long-standing Hashimoto disease. The differential includes Graves disease, factitious thyrotoxicosis, exogenous levothyroxine excess, struma ovarii, and metastatic thyroid carcinoma. In suspected suppurative thyroiditis, urgent neck CT and aspiration with culture confirm bacterial infection and guide drainage.

diagnosticTests.test: TSH and free T4 (and free T3 if T4 normal but symptoms suggest hyperthyroidism)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Establishes the current thyroid functional state. Hashimoto patients show elevated TSH with low or normal T4 in subclinical or overt hypothyroidism. Subacute and silent thyroiditis show suppressed TSH with elevated T4/T3 in the thyrotoxic phase.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw, results same or next day in most labs. Repeat at 4-6 week intervals to track the triphasic course.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Confirms autoimmune etiology. TPO antibodies positive in 95% of Hashimoto cases, 60% of postpartum thyroiditis, and 20% of subacute thyroiditis. Useful for risk-stratifying women planning pregnancy.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw. Results in 1-3 days. Once positive, repeat testing is generally not needed.

diagnosticTests.test: Erythrocyte sedimentation rate (ESR) and C-reactive protein (CRP)
diagnosticTests.purpose: Markedly elevated ESR (>50 mm/hour) supports subacute thyroiditis; normal ESR helps exclude it. CRP follows the same pattern. Normal in Hashimoto and silent thyroiditis.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Standard blood draw with results same day.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Distinguishes thyroiditis (low uptake <5%) from Graves disease (high uptake >30%) in the thyrotoxic phase. Most useful when the diagnosis is otherwise unclear.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: An oral capsule of radioactive iodine, followed by a gamma camera scan 24 hours later. Small radiation dose; avoid in pregnancy. The test takes about 20 minutes on day two.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Hashimoto shows heterogeneous hypoechoic gland with increased vascularity; subacute shows focal hypoechoic areas with reduced flow; Riedel shows infiltrative fibrosis. Also screens for nodules and thyroid lymphoma in long-standing Hashimoto.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Painless gel-and-probe scan of the anterior neck taking 15-20 minutes. No radiation. Performed by a radiologist or trained endocrinologist.

diagnosticTests.purpose: Reserved for suspected suppurative thyroiditis (Gram stain and culture), Riedel thyroiditis, or thyroid lymphoma complicating long-standing Hashimoto disease.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Performed under ultrasound guidance with a thin needle and local anesthetic. Procedure takes 10-15 minutes; cytology results within 3-5 days.

diagnosticTests.test: Neck CT or MRI in suppurative or Riedel disease
diagnosticTests.purpose: Defines abscess location and adjacent involvement in suppurative thyroiditis; demonstrates extra-thyroidal fibrosis in Riedel disease. Identifies pyriform sinus fistula in pediatric recurrent suppurative cases.
diagnosticTests.whatToExpect: Imaging takes 15-30 minutes with intravenous contrast where indicated.

treatmentOverview: Thyroiditis treatment depends on the underlying type and the current phase of disease. For the thyrotoxic phase of subacute, painless, postpartum, or drug-induced thyroiditis, beta-blockers (propranolol 20-40 mg three times daily or atenolol 25-50 mg daily) provide symptom relief while inflammation resolves. Antithyroid drugs (methimazole, propylthiouracil) are not used because the thyroid is not actively synthesizing hormone — they neither help nor harm but represent unnecessary therapy. For subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis pain, NSAIDs (ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily) provide first-line analgesia; refractory pain or persistent thyrotoxicosis warrant oral prednisone (40 mg daily tapered over 6-8 weeks), which provides rapid pain relief in 24-48 hours. For the hypothyroid phase, levothyroxine is started if TSH exceeds 10 mIU/L, if symptoms are pronounced, or if hypothyroidism persists beyond 3-6 months. Many patients in the transient hypothyroid phase recover without replacement. For established Hashimoto hypothyroidism, lifelong levothyroxine replacement is the standard — starting at 1.6 µg/kg/day in healthy adults, lower (25-50 µg) in older patients or those with cardiac disease, titrated to a TSH of 0.4-4.0 mIU/L per ATA 2014 guidelines. Combination T3/T4 therapy and desiccated thyroid extract remain controversial and are not first-line. For acute suppurative thyroiditis, intravenous broad-spectrum antibiotics (vancomycin plus a beta-lactam) and surgical drainage are required; recurrent pediatric cases need endoscopic or surgical correction of the pyriform sinus fistula. Riedel thyroiditis responds to glucocorticoids (prednisone 40 mg daily), tamoxifen, mycophenolate, or rituximab as IgG4-related disease therapy.

medicalTreatments.name: Levothyroxine (1.6 µg/kg/day in healthy adults; 25-50 µg starting dose in older or cardiac patients)
medicalTreatments.description: Standard treatment for hypothyroidism from Hashimoto disease. Synthetic T4 with consistent absorption taken on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast. Titrate every 6-8 weeks based on TSH per ATA 2014 guidelines. Target TSH 0.4-4.0 mIU/L; 0.5-2.5 in young pregnant women.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Restores euthyroid state in 95% of patients; relieves fatigue, weight, and cognitive symptoms in 70-80% when TSH is optimized.

medicalTreatments.name: Beta-blockers (propranolol 20-40 mg three times daily or atenolol 25-50 mg daily)
medicalTreatments.description: Controls symptoms of the thyrotoxic phase — palpitations, tremor, anxiety, heat intolerance. Used in subacute, painless, postpartum, and drug-induced thyroiditis. Tapered as symptoms resolve, typically over 4-8 weeks.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptom relief in 80-90% of patients within 24-48 hours. Does not alter the natural course of thyroiditis.

medicalTreatments.name: Non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs (ibuprofen 600 mg three times daily, naproxen 500 mg twice daily)
medicalTreatments.description: First-line analgesia for the painful gland of subacute thyroiditis. Most patients respond within 1-2 days. Standard duration is 1-4 weeks until pain resolves.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain resolution in 60-70% of patients within 1 week (Pearce 2003 series). Step up to corticosteroids if no improvement after 5-7 days.

medicalTreatments.name: Oral prednisone (40 mg daily tapered over 6-8 weeks)
medicalTreatments.description: For NSAID-refractory subacute thyroiditis pain or severe inflammation. Produces near-immediate pain relief and shortens the disease course in symptomatic patients. Slow taper is essential to prevent rebound.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Pain relief in 80-95% within 24-48 hours; rebound after rapid taper occurs in 15-20%.

medicalTreatments.name: Intravenous antibiotics (vancomycin plus piperacillin-tazobactam or ceftriaxone) for suppurative thyroiditis
medicalTreatments.description: Empirical broad-spectrum coverage for Staphylococcus, Streptococcus, and oral anaerobes pending culture. Combined with surgical drainage of any abscess. Treatment continued for 10-14 days based on response.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Cure rate over 90% when combined with drainage; recurrence common if pyriform sinus fistula is not corrected.

medicalTreatments.name: Rituximab or tamoxifen for Riedel thyroiditis
medicalTreatments.description: Off-label therapy for the rare fibrosing form, treated as an IgG4-related disease. Rituximab is favored when steroids fail; tamoxifen has historical efficacy in case series. Surgery reserved for airway compromise.
medicalTreatments.effectiveness: Symptomatic and radiographic improvement in 60-80% of case-series patients (Hennessey 2011).

surgicalOptions.name: Incision and drainage for suppurative thyroiditis
surgicalOptions.description: Required for any abscess that does not respond to antibiotics within 48-72 hours. Performed under general anesthesia by an ENT surgeon or head-and-neck surgeon with culture of drained material.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Symptomatic resolution in over 90% of cases; recurrence depends on correction of underlying pyriform fistula.

surgicalOptions.name: Pyriform sinus fistula obliteration
surgicalOptions.description: Definitive treatment for recurrent pediatric suppurative thyroiditis. Performed endoscopically with chemocautery or surgically with open neck dissection. Prevents recurrence in over 90% of cases.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Long-term cure of recurrent suppurative thyroiditis in 90-95%.

surgicalOptions.name: Thyroidectomy for airway compression in Riedel thyroiditis
surgicalOptions.description: Reserved for symptomatic airway, esophageal, or vascular compression that fails medical therapy. The fibrotic gland is technically difficult to remove and adjacent recurrent laryngeal nerves are at risk.
surgicalOptions.successRate: Relief of compressive symptoms in 70-85%; nerve injury rate higher than for routine thyroidectomy.

alternativeTreatments.name: Selenium supplementation (200 µg daily)
alternativeTreatments.description: Randomized trials show modest reduction in TPO antibody titers (about 30%) in early Hashimoto disease, particularly in selenium-deficient regions. Does not reverse established hypothyroidism. Reasonable adjunct but not a substitute for levothyroxine when indicated.

whySeeSpecialist: Endocrinologist referral is warranted for thyrotoxic-phase thyroiditis when the diagnosis is unclear, when there is uncertainty between thyroiditis and Graves disease, when subacute thyroiditis fails to respond to NSAIDs, when postpartum thyroiditis is recurrent across pregnancies, when checkpoint-inhibitor thyroiditis develops during cancer therapy, and when long-standing Hashimoto disease shows new nodules or rapid growth raising lymphoma concern.
doctorSelectionGuide: Look for board certification in endocrinology, experience with autoimmune thyroid disease, comfort with the RAIU vs Graves distinction in the thyrotoxic phase, and familiarity with checkpoint-inhibitor endocrinopathies if you are on cancer immunotherapy. For suppurative thyroiditis, ENT or head-and-neck surgical input is essential. Continuity matters: long-term levothyroxine titration is a multi-year relationship.
hospitalCriteria: Outpatient endocrinology service with rapid TSH, T4, and antibody testing
Access to radioactive iodine uptake testing through nuclear medicine
ENT surgical capacity for suppurative thyroiditis drainage
Multidisciplinary tumor board for suspected thyroid lymphoma in long-standing Hashimoto disease
keyFacilities: Academic endocrinology centers with autoimmune thyroid disease expertise
Cancer centers managing immune checkpoint inhibitor endocrinopathies
Pediatric otolaryngology services for recurrent suppurative thyroiditis
preventionStrategies: Monitor TSH at 6-12 weeks postpartum in women with pre-pregnancy TPO antibody positivity to detect early postpartum thyroiditis
Screen TSH every 6 months in patients on amiodarone or lithium for early detection of drug-induced thyroiditis
Avoid unnecessary iodine supplementation in iodine-sufficient populations — excess intake can trigger thyroiditis in susceptible individuals
Treat upper respiratory infections promptly to reduce, though not eliminate, the risk of subacute thyroiditis
Maintain adequate selenium intake through Brazil nuts, fish, and seafood in regions with selenium-deficient soil
lifestyleModifications: Take levothyroxine on an empty stomach 30-60 minutes before breakfast for consistent absorption
Separate levothyroxine from calcium, iron, and proton-pump inhibitors by at least 4 hours to avoid binding interactions
Maintain consistent brand or generic of levothyroxine — switching can alter absorption by 10-25%
Stop smoking — protects against Graves ophthalmopathy and cardiovascular complications of hypothyroidism
Have TSH checked at 6-8 week intervals during initial titration and every 6-12 months once stable
Inform pregnancy planning teams of thyroid status — levothyroxine requirement typically rises by 30-50% in pregnancy

recommended: Adequate but not excessive iodine intake (150 µg daily; 220 µg in pregnancy) from iodized salt, dairy, eggs, and seafood
Selenium-rich foods such as Brazil nuts (1-2 per day), tuna, sardines, and turkey
Whole grains, lean protein, vegetables, and fruit as part of a balanced diet
Adequate calcium and vitamin D intake — autoimmune thyroid disease is associated with lower bone density
avoid: Iodine megadosing from kelp supplements (often 1000-50000 µg per dose) — can trigger thyroiditis or worsen autoimmune disease
Crash diets that promote rapid weight loss and disrupt thyroid hormone equilibrium
Soy and high-fiber meals within 1 hour of levothyroxine intake — both reduce absorption
Goitrogenic foods in large quantities (raw cabbage, broccoli, cauliflower) if iodine intake is marginal

exerciseGuidelines: Regular aerobic and resistance exercise (150 minutes weekly of moderate activity plus 2 strength sessions) supports thyroid health, metabolic rate, and bone density. Defer strenuous training during the thyrotoxic phase of subacute or postpartum thyroiditis until symptoms settle. Cardiac assessment before resuming exercise is advisable in older patients with severe hyperthyroid symptoms.
dailyManagement: Take levothyroxine at the same time each day on an empty stomach
Recheck TSH every 6-8 weeks during titration and every 6-12 months once stable
Track symptoms in a simple journal during titration — energy, weight, mood, sleep, and bowel habits
Carry a medical card listing thyroid status, current levothyroxine dose, and last TSH result
Notify clinicians of all new medications — many interfere with levothyroxine absorption or metabolism
prognosis: Prognosis varies by thyroiditis type. Subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis resolves fully in 80-90% of cases over 4-6 months; 10-15% develop permanent hypothyroidism requiring lifelong replacement. Painless and postpartum thyroiditis follow the same triphasic pattern with 80% returning to euthyroid state; 20% become permanently hypothyroid and require replacement. Recurrence of postpartum thyroiditis occurs in 70% of subsequent pregnancies. Hashimoto disease is permanent — the autoimmune destruction does not reverse, but lifelong levothyroxine replacement fully controls symptoms in 95% of patients and provides normal life expectancy. Drug-induced thyroiditis usually resolves on stopping the offending agent; immune checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis often leaves permanent hypothyroidism. Acute suppurative thyroiditis has over 90% cure rate with antibiotics and drainage but recurs without fistula correction. Riedel thyroiditis is rare and progressive but responds to immunomodulatory therapy. Long-standing Hashimoto disease modestly increases the risk of thyroid lymphoma (relative risk 60 versus general population, though absolute risk remains under 0.1% per year). The decisive prognostic factor across forms is timely recognition and appropriate management of each phase.
recoveryTimeline: Subacute thyroiditis pain resolves within 1-4 weeks of starting NSAIDs or corticosteroids. The triphasic course of painless, postpartum, and subacute thyroiditis runs over 4-6 months — thyrotoxic phase 4-8 weeks, hypothyroid phase 2-6 months, recovery in most patients. Drug-induced thyroiditis resolves within 1-3 months of discontinuing the offending agent. Hashimoto disease has no recovery — levothyroxine is started and continued for life; TSH targets are reached within 3-6 months of titration in most patients.
complications: Permanent hypothyroidism after subacute, painless, or postpartum thyroiditis — occurs in 10-20% of cases
Thyroid lymphoma in long-standing Hashimoto disease — relative risk 60, absolute risk under 0.1% per year
Myxedema coma in undertreated severe hypothyroidism — life-threatening, requires intensive care
Thyroid storm in untreated thyrotoxic phase — rare but life-threatening hypermetabolic crisis
Cardiac arrhythmias including atrial fibrillation during the thyrotoxic phase, especially in older adults
Osteoporosis from untreated hypothyroidism or over-replacement; bone density should be checked periodically
Recurrent pyriform sinus fistula in pediatric suppurative thyroiditis without surgical correction

supportResources.name: American Thyroid Association — Patient Information
supportResources.description: Authoritative US patient education from the body that writes the clinical guidelines.

supportResources.name: British Thyroid Foundation
supportResources.description: UK patient community, helpline, and lifestyle resources.

supportResources.name: ATA Guidelines: Hypothyroidism and Hashimoto disease
supportResources.description: Free clinical guidance used by clinicians and accessible to motivated patients.

confusedWithConditions.name: Thyrotoxicosis (Graves Disease and other causes)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Graves disease produces sustained thyrotoxicosis with high radioactive iodine uptake (>30%), positive TSH-receptor antibodies, often ophthalmopathy, and continued hormone synthesis. Thyroiditis produces transient thyrotoxicosis with low radioactive iodine uptake (<5%) because follicles are damaged. The RAIU scan is the decisive test.

confusedWithConditions.name: Goiter
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: A goiter is enlargement of the thyroid gland from any cause — iodine deficiency, multinodular goiter, Graves disease, or thyroiditis. The diagnosis of thyroiditis adds the inflammatory and immunological features. Many thyroiditis patients have a goiter; not all goiters are thyroiditis.

confusedWithConditions.name: Hypothyroidism (Underactive Thyroid)
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Hypothyroidism is the functional state of inadequate thyroid hormone — most commonly caused by Hashimoto thyroiditis. The two terms overlap but are not identical: hypothyroidism is the deficiency; Hashimoto thyroiditis is one (the most common) cause. Treatment of either is levothyroxine replacement.

confusedWithConditions.name: Type 1 Diabetes
confusedWithConditions.keyDifference: Type 1 diabetes is a distinct autoimmune disease but shares immunogenetic susceptibility with Hashimoto thyroiditis — the two co-occur in 15-30% of either group. Patients with one autoimmune endocrinopathy should be screened periodically for the other.

relatedConditions.name: Thyrotoxicosis
relatedConditions.relevance: The thyrotoxic phase of subacute, painless, postpartum, and drug-induced thyroiditis presents as thyrotoxicosis but with low radioactive iodine uptake distinguishing it from Graves disease.

relatedConditions.name: Goiter
relatedConditions.relevance: Hashimoto thyroiditis produces a firm, rubbery, painless goiter in most patients. Riedel thyroiditis produces a hard infiltrative goiter.

relatedConditions.name: Hypothyroidism
relatedConditions.relevance: Hashimoto thyroiditis is the most common cause of hypothyroidism in iodine-sufficient regions. Subacute and postpartum thyroiditis can produce transient or permanent hypothyroidism.

relatedConditions.name: Type 1 Diabetes
relatedConditions.relevance: Shares autoimmune susceptibility. 15-30% of type 1 diabetes patients have positive TPO antibodies; routine thyroid screening is standard in their care.

faqs.question: What is thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Thyroiditis is inflammation of the thyroid gland. Multiple forms exist — Hashimoto disease (chronic autoimmune), subacute (post-viral painful), painless and postpartum (autoimmune triphasic), drug-induced, suppurative (bacterial infection), and Riedel (fibrosing). Each produces distinct symptoms and follows a different course, but all alter thyroid hormone release and require tailored treatment.

faqs.question: What are the symptoms of Hashimoto thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Hashimoto disease typically develops slowly over months to years, with fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, dry skin, hair thinning, and slowed cognition as TSH rises. Many patients also notice a painless firm goiter. Some experience an early hyperthyroid phase (hashitoxicosis) with palpitations and weight loss before settling into hypothyroidism.

faqs.question: Is thyroiditis curable?
faqs.answer: It depends on the type. Subacute, painless, and postpartum thyroiditis fully resolve in 80% of cases over 4-6 months. Drug-induced thyroiditis usually resolves on stopping the medication. Hashimoto disease is permanent but fully controlled with lifelong levothyroxine. Acute suppurative thyroiditis is cured with antibiotics and drainage.

faqs.question: How is subacute thyroiditis diagnosed?
faqs.answer: Subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis is diagnosed clinically by painful, tender thyroid swelling following a viral upper respiratory infection by 2-8 weeks. Confirmation includes a markedly elevated erythrocyte sedimentation rate (above 50 mm/hour), suppressed TSH with elevated T4 in the thyrotoxic phase, and low radioactive iodine uptake (under 5%) distinguishing it from Graves disease.

faqs.question: What causes Hashimoto thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Hashimoto thyroiditis is caused by autoimmune destruction of thyroid follicles by T cells and antibodies. Genetic factors (HLA-DR3, CTLA-4) combined with environmental triggers like high iodine intake, smoking history, pregnancy, and viral infections produce the disease. TPO antibodies are positive in 95% of cases.

faqs.question: How is thyroiditis treated?
faqs.answer: Treatment depends on the phase. The thyrotoxic phase is managed with beta-blockers; subacute thyroiditis pain with NSAIDs or oral prednisone. The hypothyroid phase is managed with levothyroxine when TSH exceeds 10 mIU/L or symptoms are pronounced. Established Hashimoto disease requires lifelong levothyroxine. Suppurative thyroiditis needs antibiotics and drainage.

faqs.question: Is postpartum thyroiditis common?
faqs.answer: Yes — postpartum thyroiditis affects 5-10% of women within 12 months of delivery. Women with pre-pregnancy TPO antibody positivity or type 1 diabetes are at higher risk (up to 25%). The condition often follows a triphasic course: thyrotoxicosis at 1-3 months, hypothyroidism at 3-9 months, and recovery in 80% of cases.

faqs.question: Can I take levothyroxine for life safely?
faqs.answer: Yes. Levothyroxine is a synthetic copy of natural thyroid hormone and, when dosed correctly, restores normal thyroid status without long-term toxicity. Patients on lifelong replacement have life expectancy equivalent to the general population. Regular TSH monitoring every 6-12 months ensures dosing remains appropriate.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Hashimoto and Graves disease?
faqs.answer: Hashimoto disease produces hypothyroidism through autoimmune destruction; antibodies target thyroid peroxidase. Graves disease produces hyperthyroidism through stimulating TSH-receptor antibodies that drive hormone overproduction. Hashimoto causes a firm painless goiter; Graves often causes eye changes (ophthalmopathy). Both are autoimmune but they produce opposite functional states.

faqs.question: How long does subacute thyroiditis last?
faqs.answer: Subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis runs a 4-6 month course. The painful thyrotoxic phase typically lasts 4-8 weeks, followed by a hypothyroid phase of 2-6 months, then recovery. 80-90% of patients return to normal thyroid function; 10-20% develop permanent hypothyroidism requiring lifelong levothyroxine.

faqs.question: Should I take selenium for Hashimoto disease?
faqs.answer: Selenium supplementation at 200 µg daily modestly reduces TPO antibody titers in some randomized trials, particularly in selenium-deficient regions. It does not reverse established hypothyroidism or replace levothyroxine. A reasonable adjunct for early disease but not a cure. Excess selenium can cause toxicity; do not exceed 400 µg daily.

faqs.question: Why does my thyroid hurt?
faqs.answer: Thyroid pain most often signals subacute (de Quervain) thyroiditis, a post-viral granulomatous inflammation. The pain radiates to the ear or jaw, the gland is exquisitely tender, and patients often run a low-grade fever. Severe sudden pain with high fever suggests suppurative thyroiditis, a surgical emergency. Hashimoto disease is usually painless.

faqs.question: Can amiodarone cause thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Amiodarone is 37% iodine by weight and causes thyroid dysfunction in 15-20% of users. Two distinct patterns exist: Type 1 thyrotoxicosis (iodine-induced hyperthyroidism in an abnormal gland) and Type 2 thyroiditis (destructive amiodarone-induced thyroiditis). Type 1 responds to antithyroid drugs; Type 2 responds to corticosteroids. Both may need amiodarone discontinuation.

faqs.question: What is checkpoint inhibitor thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Immune checkpoint inhibitors (pembrolizumab, nivolumab, ipilimumab) used in cancer immunotherapy trigger thyroiditis in 5-20% of patients depending on the regimen. Combination therapy carries the highest risk. The pattern is often a brief thyrotoxic phase followed by permanent hypothyroidism, requiring lifelong levothyroxine. Cancer immunotherapy is rarely stopped for thyroiditis alone.

faqs.question: Can thyroiditis cause weight gain?
faqs.answer: Yes — during the hypothyroid phase or once Hashimoto disease is established, weight gain of 2-10 kg is common due to slowed metabolism, fluid retention, and reduced energy expenditure. Optimal levothyroxine replacement reverses much of this gain, though not all. The thyrotoxic phase usually causes weight loss.

faqs.question: Is thyroiditis genetic?
faqs.answer: Autoimmune thyroiditis has strong genetic susceptibility — heritability estimates from twin studies reach 70%. First-degree relatives of patients have roughly 5-fold higher risk. Variants in HLA-DR3, CTLA-4, and PTPN22 are well-established susceptibility loci shared with other autoimmune diseases.

faqs.question: Can I get pregnant with thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Yes, but optimal preconception management improves outcomes. Untreated hypothyroidism in pregnancy raises miscarriage and preterm delivery risk; TPO antibody positivity increases postpartum thyroiditis risk. Levothyroxine requirement typically rises 30-50% during pregnancy and should be adjusted to TSH below 2.5 mIU/L in the first trimester.

faqs.question: When should I see an endocrinologist?
faqs.answer: See an endocrinologist when the diagnosis of thyroiditis is unclear, when subacute thyroiditis pain does not respond to NSAIDs, when postpartum thyroiditis recurs across pregnancies, when checkpoint-inhibitor thyroiditis develops during cancer therapy, when new thyroid nodules appear in long-standing Hashimoto disease, or when standard levothyroxine titration fails to control symptoms.

faqs.question: Does Hashimoto increase cancer risk?
faqs.answer: Long-standing Hashimoto thyroiditis modestly raises the risk of primary thyroid lymphoma — relative risk roughly 60 versus the general population, though absolute risk remains under 0.1% per year. Sudden enlargement of the gland or new firm nodule in a Hashimoto patient warrants urgent ultrasound and possible biopsy. Most patients never develop lymphoma.

faqs.question: Can children get thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: Yes. Hashimoto thyroiditis is the most common cause of acquired hypothyroidism in children, often presenting as a painless goiter with growth slowing. Acute suppurative thyroiditis in children frequently involves a congenital pyriform sinus fistula that requires surgical correction to prevent recurrence. Pediatric endocrinology should manage these cases.

faqs.question: What blood tests confirm thyroiditis?
faqs.answer: TSH and free T4 establish thyroid function. TPO and thyroglobulin antibodies confirm autoimmune etiology (positive in 95% of Hashimoto cases). ESR and CRP help diagnose subacute thyroiditis when markedly elevated. Radioactive iodine uptake distinguishes thyroiditis from Graves disease in the thyrotoxic phase. Ultrasound shows characteristic gland appearance.

metaDescription: Thyroiditis: Hashimoto, subacute, postpartum, drug-induced, and suppurative forms. Symptoms, diagnosis, levothyroxine treatment, and recovery timeline.
